name: Connecticut
regulatoryOwner: Connecticut Department of Energy and Environmental Protection
delegatedLocalAuthorities: State DEEP guidance anchors the answer, while transaction context determines whether the first move is records, sweep, or leak review.
buyerSellerSummary: Connecticut has clear homeowner and buyer-seller framing, which makes the transaction route especially important when records are thin.
recordLookupSummary: Use closure records, property history, and fuel evidence before assuming an absent file closes the issue.
sweepFirstSummary: A sweep-first route is often cleaner than removal talk when the site shows clues but the tank is not confirmed.
removalVsAbandonmentSummary: The proper closure path depends on what is actually there, what records exist, and whether the state process escalates beyond simple closure.
leakReportingSummary: If the question shifts from tank presence to a possible release, cleanup workflow may matter more than contractor scheduling.
insuranceCleanupSummary: Insurance and cleanup direction only becomes useful after the route is clear and evidence is stronger than suspicion.
quickAnswer: In Connecticut, start with the transaction question and the evidence stack before deciding whether this is a sweep, closure, or cleanup case.
commonTriggers: A buyer or seller needs clarity before closing.
Property history suggests prior oil heat but paperwork is incomplete.
The parties need to separate suspicion from confirmed leak concern.
cautions: Do not overstate contamination from a general page.
Do not treat old heating history as proof of a current tank.
Do not skip records review when the transaction is still live.
strongestTriggerStates: Buyer-seller timing is live and the parties still do not know if this is suspicion, a confirmed tank, or a leak concern.
Old oil-heat history appears in the property file, but closure proof is thin.
Transaction urgency is pushing the parties toward quotes before the evidence stack is clear.
firstMoves: Start with DEEP guidance, seller paperwork, and prior fuel history before widening the route into removal or cleanup.
Use a sweep when the site shows clues but the record stack does not confirm the tank.
Separate tank-presence questions from release questions so the next call matches the actual route.
documentTargets: Seller disclosure language, DEEP-related closure paperwork, and any prior contractor record.
Fuel conversion or burner replacement documents that help date the move away from oil heat.
Any cleanup, spill, or no-further-action document showing the issue already moved beyond ordinary closure.
escalationSignals: Odor, sheen, stained soil, or contractor notes suggesting release concern rather than simple verification.
A file that references cleanup, spill response, or environmental review.
Facts confirming the tank is still in place and changing the route from suspicion to closure planning.

sourceStack.title: Connecticut DEEP Residential Home Heating Oil Tanks FAQ
